claude.name: Executive Power Consolidation
claude.definition: The ongoing structural dynamic by which the Trump White House, cabinet principals, and aligned political operatives expand presidential authority over federal agencies, regulatory bodies, and spending — tracked weekly via executive orders, agency restructurings, DOGE actions, personnel replacements, and congressional or judicial pushback.

claude.name: AI Dominance Race
claude.definition: The sustained competition among major AI developers — including OpenAI, xAI, Google DeepMind, Microsoft AI, and their investors — to capture talent, compute, regulatory favor, and commercial deployment at scale, tracked weekly via model releases, funding rounds, partnership announcements, and federal AI policy moves.

claude.name: Conservative Judicial Entrenchment
claude.definition: The long-running structural dynamic by which the Federalist Society network, dark-money infrastructure, and aligned White House actors consolidate a conservative supermajority on federal courts through nominations, confirmations, and landmark rulings — tracked weekly via court decisions, nomination proceedings, and Federalist Society signaling.

claude.name: Big Tech Regulatory Arbitrage
claude.definition: The ongoing dynamic by which dominant technology platforms — Apple, Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and Oracle — negotiate, resist, or co-opt federal and state regulatory frameworks covering antitrust, data privacy, content moderation, and AI liability, tracked weekly via litigation outcomes, lobbying activity, congressional hearings, and agency rulemaking.

claude.name: Private Capital Allocation Shift
claude.definition: The structural dynamic by which major alternative asset managers, sovereign-adjacent funds, and billionaire investors — including Blackstone, Apollo, Founders Fund, and their networks — redirect capital flows across private equity, credit, real estate, and emerging technology, reshaping industry ownership and policy leverage, tracked weekly via deal announcements, fund raises, and macro portfolio shifts.

claude.name: Defense-Industrial Expansion
claude.definition: The sustained dynamic by which the Department of Defense, SpaceX, Palantir, and allied defense contractors expand military capability through increased procurement, AI-enabled warfighting systems, space-based assets, and allied interoperability agreements — tracked weekly via Pentagon budget actions, contract awards, and weapons program milestones.

claude.name: Monetary Policy Stress Cycle
claude.definition: The ongoing tension between the Federal Reserve's interest rate and balance-sheet decisions and the fiscal pressures generated by Treasury policy, congressional spending, and executive economic demands — tracked weekly via Fed statements, Treasury issuance data, inflation indicators, and public friction between monetary and fiscal authorities.

claude.name: Information Environment Contestation
claude.definition: The structural dynamic by which competing actors — including X Corp, Meta, legacy media outlets like The Washington Post, and politically aligned networks — fight for narrative control, audience reach, and platform governance rules across US political and cultural discourse, tracked weekly via platform policy changes, ownership moves, audience metrics, and high-profile content moderation decisions.

claude.name: Energy Transition Power Struggle
claude.definition: The ongoing contest among fossil fuel incumbents, nuclear innovators, renewable developers, and federal regulators over the pace, financing, and infrastructure of US energy system transformation — tracked weekly via regulatory approvals, DOE funding decisions, grid investment announcements, and legislative or executive actions affecting energy markets.

claude.name: Institutional Norm Erosion
claude.definition: The cross-sector dynamic by which established procedural, ethical, and legal norms governing federal agencies, public health bodies, religious institutions, and civil society organizations are challenged, rewritten, or bypassed by insurgent actors — tracked weekly via agency policy reversals, legal challenges to administrative authority, public health mandate conflicts, and civil society mobilization responses.

chatgpt.name: Executive Control & Purge
chatgpt.definition: Tracks the consolidation or fragmentation of presidential control over the federal bureaucracy via appointments, removals, agency reorgs, OMB actions, executive orders, and compliance/resistance by departments and independent agencies.

chatgpt.name: Fiscal-Monetary Bargaining
chatgpt.definition: Tracks how Treasury, the Fed, and Congress shape macro conditions through debt/deficit politics, taxation, sanctions/financial restrictions, interest-rate policy, bank regulation, and liquidity backstops as reflected in weekly policy signals and market reactions.

chatgpt.name: Judicial Pipeline & Doctrine
chatgpt.definition: Tracks the production and enforcement of durable legal power through judicial nominations, court rulings, strategic litigation, amicus networks, and regulatory-jurisprudential feedback loops that expand or constrain state and corporate authority.

chatgpt.name: AI Platformization & Control
chatgpt.definition: Tracks the race to build and govern frontier AI platforms—models, chips, data, cloud, and distribution—via product launches, compute deals, safety standards, licensing regimes, and competitive positioning among major labs and incumbents.

chatgpt.name: Data, Identity & Surveillance
chatgpt.definition: Tracks the expansion and contestation of identity systems and surveillance capabilities—biometrics, KYC/AML, ad-tech, device ecosystems, govtech procurement, and intelligence partnerships—through new programs, enforcement actions, and major breaches or disclosures.

chatgpt.name: Information Dominance & Agenda
chatgpt.definition: Tracks how narratives and attention are shaped through platform policy, algorithmic amplification, media ownership/editorial moves, influencer ecosystems, and coordinated political communications measured by content moderation changes, messaging campaigns, and audience shifts.

chatgpt.name: Defense-Industrial Mobilization
chatgpt.definition: Tracks the allocation of national security resources and authority through budgets, procurement awards, readiness posture, export controls, space and cyber operations, and the integration of private contractors into defense planning and execution.

chatgpt.name: Energy Transition & Security
chatgpt.definition: Tracks the struggle over energy abundance and resilience—oil/gas policy, nuclear and fusion bets, grid buildout, permitting, climate regulation, and strategic resource constraints—via federal/state rulemaking, corporate capex, and supply-chain disruptions.

chatgpt.name: Capital Allocation & Capture
chatgpt.definition: Tracks how large asset managers, private equity, venture capital, and major corporates steer the real economy through dealmaking, buybacks, roll-ups, bankruptcy workouts, lobbying, and regulatory arbitrage visible in weekly transactions and policy pressure.

chatgpt.name: Coalition Machinery & Elections
chatgpt.definition: Tracks the ongoing construction and disciplining of political coalitions through party apparatus control, super PAC funding, voter targeting, legal ballot access fights, and turnout operations as evidenced by fundraising flows, endorsements, and campaign infrastructure moves.

grok.name: Executive Reorganization Drive
grok.definition: Ongoing restructuring of federal agencies and policy priorities under the Trump administration, tracked via cabinet actions, DOGE reports, and departmental directives involving HHS, Treasury, Defense, and White House operations.

grok.name: AI Supremacy Competition
grok.definition: Rivalry among leading AI firms for technological dominance and talent, tracked through product launches, partnerships, investments, and regulatory filings by OpenAI, xAI, Google, Microsoft, and others.

grok.name: Judicial Doctrine Evolution
grok.definition: Shifts in Supreme Court rulings and conservative legal network influence on nominations and cases, tracked via opinions, briefs, and Federalist Society events involving justices and Leo's organizations.

grok.name: Monetary Policy Balancing
grok.definition: Federal Reserve adjustments to interest rates and financial regulations amid fiscal pressures, tracked through FOMC meetings, statements, and Treasury coordination under Powell and Bessent.

grok.name: Big Tech Regulation Battles
grok.definition: Antitrust scrutiny and lobbying by dominant tech platforms against government oversight, tracked via FTC/DOJ actions, congressional hearings, and CEO statements from Amazon, Apple, Google, Meta.

grok.name: Defense Contractor Expansion
grok.definition: Growth in military contracts and tech integrations for national security, tracked through DoD awards, SpaceX launches, Palantir deployments, and Blue Origin developments under Hegseth.

grok.name: Media Narrative Shaping
grok.definition: Competition for public discourse control among owned platforms, tracked via content policy changes, algorithmic shifts, and coverage controversies at Washington Post, X Corp, and Meta.

grok.name: Fusion Energy Acceleration
grok.definition: Advances in nuclear fusion and alternative energy investments, tracked through funding rounds, milestones, and policy support for Helion Energy and related ventures.

grok.name: Conservative Funding Networks
grok.definition: Flows of dark money and strategic philanthropy through legal and political vehicles, tracked via donor disclosures, grants, and campaign expenditures by Marble Freedom Trust, CRC Advisors, and similar entities.

gemini.name: Executive Agency Restructuring
gemini.definition: The coordinated effort to dismantle, defund, or reorganize federal administrative state capabilities, tracked through executive orders, DOGE initiatives, and cabinet-level deregulation policies.

gemini.name: AI Capability Scaling
gemini.definition: The rapid development, compute acquisition, and commercial deployment of frontier artificial intelligence models, tracked through product releases, data center build-outs, and capital fundraising by leading AI labs.

gemini.name: Conservative Legal Dominance
gemini.definition: The systematic reshaping of US constitutional interpretation and regulatory law, tracked through Supreme Court rulings, appellate appointments, and strategic litigation funded by dark money networks.

gemini.name: Macroeconomic Capital Allocation
gemini.definition: The ongoing management of US monetary policy, sovereign debt, and private capital flows, tracked through Federal Reserve rate decisions, Treasury issuances, and massive alternative asset deployments.

gemini.name: Defense and Space Privatization
gemini.definition: The ongoing shift of critical aerospace, intelligence, and military capabilities to commercial technology contractors, tracked through major DOD/NASA contract awards and successful private rocket launches.

gemini.name: Platform Information Control
gemini.definition: The mediation of political discourse and public narrative by concentrated digital platforms and billionaire-owned media properties, tracked through content moderation policy changes, algorithm updates, and platform acquisitions.

gemini.name: Cloud Infrastructure Consolidation
gemini.definition: The lock-in of global enterprise and government data architectures by mega-cap technology firms, tracked through cloud market share shifts, massive enterprise software contracts, and data center expansions.

gemini.name: Advanced Energy Commercialization
gemini.definition: The pursuit and scaling of next-generation energy solutions to meet soaring compute and grid demands, tracked through investments in nuclear fusion, fission deployments, and advanced power infrastructure.

gemini.name: Techno-Capital Political Realignment
gemini.definition: The convergence of Silicon Valley wealth and conservative political structures to deregulate emerging industries, tracked through billionaire campaign contributions, super PAC activities, and tech lobbying outcomes.

rankings.reasoning: Model A achieves the strongest balance across all four criteria. Coverage is comprehensive, spanning executive control, fiscal-monetary dynamics, judicial power, AI, surveillance, information, defense, energy, capital allocation, and electoral machinery — the full actor pool including Congress, Fed, courts, corporates, and political infrastructure. Distinctiveness is high: each sequence has a clearly bounded domain with minimal overlap (e.g., DPS-02 fiscal-monetary vs DPS-09 capital allocation are genuinely separate). Operationalizability is strong because each sequence names specific trackable signals (OMB actions, FOMC signals, procurement awards, fundraising flows). Breadth is excellent — sequences are defined at a structural level rather than anchored to specific named actors or companies, making them durable beyond any single news cycle or administration.

Model C is conceptually clean and structurally sound, with well-named sequences that capture most major power domains. It correctly identifies cloud infrastructure consolidation (DPS-07) as a distinct sequence that Model B misses. However, it shows moderate overlap between DPS-02 (AI Capability Scaling) and DPS-07 (Cloud Infrastructure Consolidation), and between DPS-05 (Defense/Space Privatization) and DPS-09 (Techno-Capital Political Realignment). Coverage is slightly weaker than Model A — electoral machinery and surveillance/identity systems are absent. Breadth is good but some sequences (DPS-09 especially) lean toward current-moment actors rather than structural dynamics, slightly reducing durability.

Model B is the weakest of the three primarily on breadth and distinctiveness. Several sequences are anchored too tightly to specific named entities (Helion Energy, DOGE, SpaceX, Marble Freedom Trust), making them narrow trackers of particular actors rather than structural power dynamics. DPS-08 (Fusion Energy Acceleration) is far too narrow to constitute a major US power sequence at this time. Coverage gaps are notable: no electoral/coalition machinery sequence, no surveillance/identity sequence, and fiscal-monetary and capital allocation are conflated. Overlap between DPS-02 (AI Supremacy) and DPS-05 (Big Tech Regulation) is significant. Operationalizability is adequate but the actor-specific framing limits generalizability beyond the current moment.

Best overall coverage and operationalizability: it spans executive power, courts, tech regulation, capital, defense, monetary/fiscal tension, information environment, and energy transition, plus a cross-cutting “institutional norm erosion” lens. Sequences are mostly distinct and trackable weekly via clear indicators (EOs, rulings, contracts, Fed/Treasury signals, platform policy changes, regulatory actions). Breadth is strong because categories generalize beyond the named anchor actors.

Strong breadth and decent distinctiveness, adding useful structural lenses (cloud infrastructure consolidation; techno-capital political realignment) that generalize beyond specific firms. However, it has more overlap/entanglement among tech-related tracks (AI scaling, platform information control, cloud consolidation, techno-capital realignment) and its “macroeconomic capital allocation” bundles multiple dynamics (Fed, Treasury, private capital) that can be harder to disentangle week-to-week than A’s more separated tracks.

Good operationalizability and reasonable coverage, but weaker distinctiveness and breadth: “Conservative funding networks” substantially overlaps with “Judicial doctrine evolution,” and “Fusion energy acceleration” is narrower than broader energy-transition/advanced-energy power struggles. It also omits some cross-cutting institutional dynamics (e.g., norm erosion) and has less general framing beyond the specific anchor entities compared with A and C.
Best coverage with unique additions like surveillance and elections; highly distinct, operational via policy signals and deals; broadest neutrality beyond current anchors.
Strong coverage of key dynamics, very explicit weekly trackers, distinct; slightly less broad due to admin-specific references reducing generality.
Good core coverage but narrower tech focus (e.g., cloud, fusion), only 9 sequences, some overlaps (capital/techno), less operational for weekly shifts.

Offers the best structural abstraction. The sequences are politically and temporally neutral (e.g., 'Judicial Pipeline' rather than 'Conservative Entrenchment'), providing excellent breadth and durability while remaining highly operationalizable for weekly tracking across the full actor pool.

Strong coverage and operational tracking metrics, but embeds specific factions and current administrations directly into the core definitions (e.g., 'Trump White House', 'Conservative Judicial Entrenchment'), making it less structurally durable and broad than Model B.

Weakest breadth and durability. The sequences are highly over-fitted to current anchor neurons, specific individuals, and niche industries (e.g., naming Helion Energy, Pete Hegseth, and Scott Bessent directly), failing to establish generalized structural dynamics.

phase_sequences.name: Executive Power Consolidation
phase_sequences.definition: The expansion or contraction of presidential control over the federal bureaucracy through executive orders, agency reorganizations, personnel purges, DOGE-style efficiency initiatives, and OMB directives — tracked weekly via White House actions, departmental compliance or resistance, and legal challenges to executive authority.

phase_sequences.name: AI Dominance Race
phase_sequences.definition: The sustained competition among frontier AI labs, cloud hyperscalers, and their investors to capture talent, compute, data, regulatory favor, and commercial deployment at scale — tracked weekly via model releases, compute deals, funding rounds, partnership announcements, safety governance moves, and federal AI policy actions.

phase_sequences.name: Judicial Pipeline & Doctrinal Shift
phase_sequences.definition: The production of durable legal power through judicial nominations, confirmations, Supreme Court and appellate rulings, strategic litigation, amicus networks, and dark-money-funded vetting infrastructure — tracked weekly via court decisions, nomination proceedings, Federalist Society signaling, and regulatory-jurisprudential feedback loops.

phase_sequences.name: Fiscal-Monetary Stress Cycle
phase_sequences.definition: The ongoing tension between Federal Reserve interest-rate and balance-sheet decisions and the fiscal pressures generated by Treasury debt management, congressional spending, and executive economic demands — tracked weekly via FOMC statements, Treasury issuance data, inflation indicators, yield curve movements, and public friction between monetary and fiscal authorities.

phase_sequences.name: Capital Allocation & Private Power
phase_sequences.definition: The redirection of capital flows by major alternative asset managers, venture funds, private equity firms, and billionaire investors across real estate, credit, technology, and strategic industries — reshaping ownership structures and policy leverage — tracked weekly via deal announcements, fund raises, roll-ups, buybacks, and lobbying tied to portfolio interests.

phase_sequences.name: Big Tech Regulatory Arbitrage
phase_sequences.definition: The dynamic by which dominant technology platforms negotiate, resist, or co-opt federal and state regulatory frameworks covering antitrust, data privacy, content moderation, cloud lock-in, and AI liability — tracked weekly via FTC/DOJ actions, congressional hearings, litigation outcomes, lobbying disclosures, and major enterprise or government contract awards.

phase_sequences.name: Defense-Industrial Expansion
phase_sequences.definition: The allocation of national security resources and the integration of private technology contractors into defense, intelligence, and space operations — tracked weekly via Pentagon budget actions, procurement awards, SpaceX and Palantir-class contract milestones, export controls, and readiness posture shifts.

phase_sequences.name: Information Environment Contestation
phase_sequences.definition: The competition for narrative control, audience capture, and platform governance rules among digital platforms, billionaire-owned media properties, legacy outlets, and politically aligned communication networks — tracked weekly via content moderation policy changes, algorithmic shifts, ownership moves, audience metrics, and coordinated messaging campaigns.

phase_sequences.name: Energy Transition & Security
phase_sequences.definition: The contest among fossil fuel incumbents, nuclear and fusion ventures, renewable developers, and federal regulators over the pace, financing, and infrastructure of US energy system transformation to meet surging compute and grid demands — tracked weekly via regulatory approvals, DOE funding decisions, corporate capex announcements, permitting actions, and legislative moves affecting energy markets.

phase_sequences.name: Coalition Machinery & Electoral Infrastructure
phase_sequences.definition: The construction, funding, and disciplining of political coalitions through party apparatus control, super PAC expenditures, dark-money networks, voter targeting operations, and legal ballot-access fights — tracked weekly via fundraising flows, endorsement patterns, campaign infrastructure moves, and donor-network activations.
